serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of raw arugula is approximately 20 g (1 loosely packed cup), commonly used as a salad base or garnish.

nutrient_source.title: Arugula, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169387)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo database (data_source: hpb, confidence 0.95). USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 169387 is an exact match for raw arugula. Core macros and micronutrients are consistent between both sources. USDA values used as primary reference; HPB values confirmed alignment.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Non-Starchy Leafy Vegetables — Analogous Estimate Based on University of Sydney GI Database Low-GI Leafy Vegetable Category
gi_source.source_match_notes: Arugula is not individually listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. A GI of approximately 15 is estimated based on the established low-GI category for non-starchy leafy green vegetables (e.g., lettuce, spinach, watercress), all of which have GI values in the range of 10–20 per the University of Sydney GI Database. With only 3.65 g carbohydrate per 100 g and a 20 g serving, the glycemic load per serving is approximately 0.1, confirming negligible glycemic impact. This estimate is consistent with peer-reviewed literature on cruciferous and leafy vegetables.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (27 mg/100 g), high potassium (369 mg/100 g), high calcium (160 mg/100 g), and dietary nitrates supporting vasodilation
hypertension.guidance: Arugula is an excellent choice for hypertensive patients: its low sodium content, high potassium-to-sodium ratio, and naturally occurring dietary nitrates support blood pressure reduction consistent with DASH dietary principles; patients on warfarin should maintain consistent arugula intake due to its high vitamin K content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (3.65 g/100 g), low sugar (2.05 g/100 g), moderate dietary fibre (1.6 g/100 g), and negligible glycemic load per serving (GL ≈ 0.1)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Arugula is highly suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes: its negligible glycemic load, low carbohydrate density, and fibre content support postprandial blood glucose control; patients should avoid pairing with high-sugar dressings that would significantly raise the overall glycemic load of the meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol-free (0 mg/100 g), low saturated fat (0.09 g/100 g), high potassium (369 mg/100 g), folate (97 mcg DFE/100 g), and anti-inflammatory glucosinolates
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Arugula is cardioprotective: it is cholesterol-free, very low in saturated fat, and rich in potassium and folate, which support vascular health and homocysteine regulation; its glucosinolates and dietary nitrates provide additional anti-inflammatory and endothelial benefits relevant to cardiovascular disease prevention.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory sulforaphane and glucosinolates, vitamin C (15 mg/100 g), calcium (160 mg/100 g)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Arugula's sulforaphane and glucosinolate content provide documented anti-inflammatory benefits relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; patients on methotrexate should maintain consistent intake to avoid fluctuations in vitamin K levels.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Arugula (Eruca sativa), also known as rocket, rucola, or roquette, is a raw leafy green vegetable originating from the Mediterranean region. It is characterised by its dark green, deeply lobed leaves with a distinctively peppery, slightly bitter flavour and tender texture. Arugula is consumed raw as a salad base, pizza topping, pasta garnish, or sandwich ingredient, and is a staple of Italian and broader Mediterranean cuisine. Nutritionally, raw arugula is extremely low in calories (25 kcal/100 g) and carbohydrates (3.65 g/100 g), making it suitable for low-calorie, ketogenic, and diabetic dietary patterns. It provides 2.58 g protein, 0.66 g fat, 1.6 g dietary fibre, and 2.05 g sugar per 100 g. It is an excellent source of calcium (160 mg/100 g), potassium (369 mg/100 g), folate (97 mcg DFE/100 g), and vitamin A (119 mcg RAE/100 g), and contains meaningful amounts of vitamin C (15 mg/100 g), iron (1.46 mg/100 g), and magnesium (47 mg/100 g). Its very low sodium content (27 mg/100 g) and high potassium-to-sodium ratio make it particularly beneficial for hypertensive individuals following DASH dietary guidelines. Arugula is a cruciferous vegetable containing glucosinolates and sulforaphane, compounds with documented anti-inflammatory and potential chemoprotective properties. Its dietary nitrates act as natural vasodilators, supporting endothelial function and cardiovascular health. Arugula is naturally cholesterol-free, gluten-free, vegan, halal, and kosher, and is appropriate for virtually all dietary patterns. It has a negligible glycemic index (estimated GI ≈ 15) and glycemic load per serving (GL ≈ 0.1), making it one of the most diabetes-friendly vegetables available.
common_user_queries: What are the health benefits of arugula?
Can I eat arugula on a keto diet?
Is arugula good for blood pressure?
What is the glycemic index of arugula?
Is rocket the same as arugula?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169387), an exact match for raw arugula, cross-confirmed with Singapore HPB Foodo database at 0.95 confidence. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are directly available from the approved primary source. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database category for non-starchy leafy greens, clearly documented.
notes: cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): arugula is a plant food and naturally contains no cholesterol per USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 169387. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero): arugula is a plant food and contains no vitamin B12. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero): raw arugula contains no vitamin D per USDA FoodData Central. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (naturally zero): raw arugula contains no trans fats. GI value of 15 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database low-GI leafy vegetable category; arugula is not individually listed. Saturated fat (0.09 g), monounsaturated fat (0.05 g), and polyunsaturated fat (0.32 g) values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 169387. Ontology codes: FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03301710 and FoodEx2 code A00LC are used as defensible anchors; SNOMED CT code 226723005 is included as a substance-level anchor. No mandatory multilingual names required as arugula is a generic global ingredient of Mediterranean origin, not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish requiring mandatory local-language coverage under G6.
